title: Composite format strings should not lead to unexpected behavior at runtime
description: Composite format strings should not lead to unexpected behavior at runtimeAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalreliabilityBugWhy is this an issue?More InfoComposite format strings in C# are evaluated at runtime, which means they are not verified by the compiler. Introducing an ill-formed format item, or indexing mismatch can lead to unexpected behaviors or runtime errors. The purpose of this rule is to perform static validation on composite format strings used in various string formatting functions to ensure their correct usage.
recommendation: Replace with the recommended alternative approach.

noncompliant: string result = String.Format("{0} {1}"); // Noncompliant: missing arguments causes FormatException
string result = String.Format("{2}", arg0, arg1); // Noncompliant: index out of range
compliant: string result = String.Format("{0} {1}", arg0, arg1); // Compliant: correct number of arguments

detection_conditions: Number of placeholders does not match number of arguments provided
triggering_conditions: String.Format() call has more placeholders than arguments (e.g., {0} {1} but only 1 arg)
String.Format() call has placeholder index out of range (e.g., {2} but only 2 args)
String.Format() call has fewer arguments than required by placeholders
exclusion_conditions: Correct number of arguments matching placeholder count
Using string interpolation ($"{var}") instead of String.Format
Inside comments or string literals in documentation
never_trigger_on: String.Format() with correct argument count matching placeholders
Logging or debug output statements where format is intentionally empty
checks.reasoning: This rule prevents runtime FormatException errors by validating that composite format strings have the correct number of arguments. The check_composite_format_string_lead function parses String.Format calls, counts placeholders in the format string, determines the expected argument count based on max placeholder index, counts actual arguments provided, and reports violations when counts don't match to prevent unexpected runtime behavior.

validation_examples.reason: Placeholder {2} requires 3 arguments but only 2 provided
